steps.title: Spot the core prediction
steps.content: Charles and Stephens's argument hinges on one key claim: informed voters are more likely to vote because they're more confident in evaluating candidates. The decisive test is thus whether high-information voters actually vote more than low-information voters.

steps.title: Find the data supporting this prediction
steps.content: Choice A directly shows that high-information voters vote more frequently than low-information voters at every point on the political orientation scale, confirming the prediction. Choices B, C, and D either examine low-information voters in isolation, focus on political identity rather than voting likelihood, or report statistics for specific subgroups instead of making the key high-versus-low comparison. That matches choice A — A is correct.
